metric_value: Bookings, billings, and shipping curves skewed toward end of operating period; shape resembles a hockey stick. Pattern exists across industries including commuters, fast food, agriculture, and investment management.
notes: Casale coins or popularizes the term 'hockey stick' for end-period concentration in tech company quarterly operations; notes the origin of the term is 'lost in industrial folklore'.

metric_name: Shift from annual to quarterly operating cycle — historical context
metric_value: 1950s: corporate America on annual cycles; quarterly earnings estimates and portfolio measurements were 'barely dreamed of'. Shift to quarterly competitively imposed on the computer industry.
notes: Continues the EVP-2 treadmill analysis with historical framing. Casale attributes the shift to quarterly cycles to competitive pressure rather than internal management choice.

metric_name: Four small hockey sticks vs. one large — quarterly cycle advantages
metric_value: Quarterly cycle superior to annual: (1) salesperson given a quarter closes faster than given a year; (2) factory flow smoother with 4 peak cycles vs. 1; (3) management gets more accurate quarterly business outlook; (4) improves cash flow and reduces need for incremental labor/plant.
notes: Casale makes an explicit efficiency argument for the quarterly cycle — framing the hockey stick as rational and beneficial, not pathological per se.

metric_value: Flat booking/shipping curve works only if: (1) monopoly product; (2) demand greatly exceeds supply; (3) product life measured in years; (4) control of raw material supply; (5) labor surplus at low wages. These conditions are exceptions in high-tech.
notes: Casale uses 'pancake curve' as opposite of hockey stick; argues smooth operations are structurally impossible in competitive tech markets.

metric_name: Two methods to flatten the hockey stick
metric_value: Method 1 (Stick): Manage salesforce to optimize factory flow; goal is low-cost production. Method 2 (Carrot): Manage factory to maximize near-term sales; goal is market share leadership. Both are variants on the same theme.
notes: Casale's 'stick vs. carrot' framework for hockey-stick management prefigures S&OP (Sales and Operations Planning) thinking that became standard in the 1990s.

metric_name: Pitfalls of downsizing for backlog building (stick approach)
metric_value: Risks: (1) loss of market share as competitors deliver what you forego; (2) customer satisfaction slips if deliveries stretch; (3) newly announced competitive product can melt backlog and devalue WIP/finished goods inventories.
notes: Casale warns explicitly against 'the backlog trap' — excess backlog is 'a snare and delusion'. Cross-reference future EVP on backlog.

metric_value: Datapoint's compensation plan until recently was heavily skewed towards bookings; resulted in large booking numbers that did not convert to shipments or revenues.
notes: Casale identifies compensation plan design as the root cause of Datapoint's accounting problems — a structural issue rather than individual malfeasance.

metric_value: In early years: compensation skewed to customer payment; improved receivables but 'turned salesmen into dunning agents'; first months of quarter spent collecting rather than selling.
notes: Used as counter-example to Datapoint's booking-skew; illustrates that over-correction to customer payment creates its own operational problems.

metric_name: Tandem — booked shipments not resulting in revenues
metric_value: Even booked shipments did not always result in immediate revenues; fiscal 1982 results restated downward.
notes: Casale notes the distinction between booking → shipment → revenue → cash collection as four distinct conversion steps, each with failure modes.

metric_value: Perfect compensation plan does not exist; changing market conditions for products and salespeople prevent it. Plans must be continuously tweaked with special incentives for early collection, monthly performance, and specific products.
notes: Casale draws on 'personal experience' to argue for adaptive compensation design rather than fixed formulas.

metric_name: Atari/Warner — annual hockey stick investment magnitude
metric_value: Annual hockey stick: last few weeks of Q4 determine the entire year. Warner Communications Atari Division recent debacle cited as investment-magnitude example.
notes: Atari's 1982 crash (Atari Video Game Collapse) used to illustrate the investment risk of annual versus quarterly cycles. Stock cited at time of analysis but price not given.

metric_name: Customer buying cycle drives compensation plan length
metric_value: Monthly compensation plans work only if customer buying cycle is roughly same length; 'to a very large extent, the customer drives the plan.'
notes: Key insight: the operating cycle of the seller must align with the buying cycle of the customer — an early articulation of customer-driven sales cycle management.

metric_name: Investment relevance — daily vs. weekly vs. quarterly cycles
metric_value: Daily cycles give investors updated information every day; weekly (e.g., money supply statistics) 52 data points per year; quarterly cycle imposes peculiar accounting implications specific to tech sector.
notes: Casale frames the investment relevance of cycle length as an information-frequency problem; quarterly cycle is the worst of both worlds for investors.

metric_name: Prediction: booking/billing practices at Tandem will continue to generate controversy
metric_value: Fiscal 1982 results at Tandem being restated downwards; further analysis of operating rationale behind events at Tandem and Datapoint to follow in EVP-4.
notes: Casale is writing contemporaneously with the Tandem controversy; implies more disclosures to come.

metric_name: Prediction: booking/billing practices at Tandem will continue to generate controversy
Stock prices at time of publication (Jan 5 1983)
notes: Published market prices provide valuation context; all three companies in computing sector, all below prior-year highs due to accounting controversies and/or sector weakness.

metric_value: Pattern appears in: commuter traffic (2 per day), McDonald's lunch/dinner rushes, quarter-end portfolio window dressing, agricultural harvest cycles. Hockey stick is a universal human organizational phenomenon.
notes: Casale broadens the hockey stick concept beyond tech to establish it as a natural law of organized human activity, reinforcing his argument that it cannot be eliminated.

metric_name: Accounting implications of quarterly cycle — teaser for EVP-4
metric_value: The quarterly cycle in the computer industry has peculiar aspects related to accounting practices; to be analyzed in next two Executive Viewpoints.
notes: EVP-3 ends with a forward reference to EVP-4's P&L analysis; the hockey stick is framed as having both operational and accounting/financial reporting implications.
